content:
https://www.ipetitions.com/petition/mathematics-is-not-redundant

Simona Paoli wrote:

The proposals represent an attack on pure mathematics research in general and a
specific threat to the category theory research represented at Leicester and to the LMS funded Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory network which has its main node at Leicester.

The University of Leicester wants to lay off all 8 of its pure mathematicians, including all 3 women with permanent positions, and then rehire just 3 of them - to do teaching, but not research. They insanely claim:

to ensure a future research identity in AI, computational modelling, digitalisation and data science requires ceasing research in Pure Mathematics

The category theorist Simona Paoli is one of those who will lose her job.

Protest by signing the petition!

John Baez said: They insanely claim:

to ensure a future research identity in AI, computational modelling, digitalisation and data science requires ceasing research in Pure Mathematics

that is some statement, isn't it? i haven't heard a better statement since the monty python times. put a cart in front of the horse, and then ask him to go backwards.

Yes, but it looks like the guy behind this has a knack for such statements:

Some 60 people could be made redundant at the end of a current consultation and staff have been angered by vice-chancellor Professor Nishan Canagarajah’s assertion that the move is needed to “continue to deliver excellence.”

continue to deliver excellence

These buzzwords bother me so much, and it just becomes creepier with context.

They sound like statements that the AIs would make after taking over...

I'm sincerely anguished about this. It's nasty on so many levels. Not only you are put in a precarious spot but you're told it's "for excellence" like they'll be better off without you. It's even worse than "cutting costs". Nobody deserves this and knowing what a great researcher Simona is only compounds it.
Do you know whether this university has defunded or weakly supports humanities?

Ivan Di Liberti said:

Do you know whether this university has defunded or weakly supports humanities?

Here's the full statement: https://www.uculeicester.org.uk/ucu/first-statement-on-threatened-compulsory-redundancies/

The affected staff are in five academic departments – English; Business; Informatics; Mathematics & Actuarial Science; and Neuroscience, Psychology & Behaviour – and three professional services units – Education Services; Student & Information Services; and Estates & Digital Services.
It's so surreal. It almost sounds like a deliberate sabotage of the University as an institution.

Ivan Di Liberti said:

Do you know whether this university has defunded or weakly supports humanities?

I think this kind of treatment of humanities departments is so common nobody notices it...

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

It's so surreal. It almost sounds like a deliberate sabotage of the University as an institution.

I would wager that it is perhaps deliberate sabotage. Corporations lobby for austerity, which forces universities to cut back on fundamental research and make profits through teaching and contracts with private industry, thus making money for the corporations involved: cheap research and less taxes. I'm not sure if this is the case here, but this is definitely a pervasive issue in Canada, in my opinion.
It's important information here that Leicester tried to do almost this exact thing before, in (I think) 2016, and in the end mostly backed down after a massive fight
The cynic in me wants to say that everybody else in the UK doesn't do this only because all the departments left standing have learned, the painful way, to continually bring in grant funding
I think there's very few universities that keep their maths department purely out of a sense that a university ought to have a maths department
In the UK, that bus has already left....

Jules Hedges said:

Ivan Di Liberti said:

Do you know whether this university has defunded or weakly supports humanities?

I think this kind of treatment of humanities departments is so common nobody notices it...

Do you have any evidence for such a bold statement? Are humanities departments shrinking in UK in the last ten years?

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

It's so surreal. It almost sounds like a deliberate sabotage of the University as an institution.

Isn't it more a rebranding? Maybe I misunderstood, but isn't the plan to reopen these positions in more trendy topics? For example AI-oriented maths.

There are still students currently on courses which are taught by these people, and the plan is to rehire three members of teaching staff for all pure maths courses? A university (ostensibly) serves higher education and research; how can a move which directly harms both of these goals be 'rebranding'? As if students weren't already dealing with enough shit...

Ivan Di Liberti said:

Do you have any evidence for such a bold statement?

No

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

It's so surreal. It almost sounds like a deliberate sabotage of the University as an institution.

well, they did it before when they closed the maths Dept in Bangor in 2007, Ronnie Brown, Tim Porter and colleagues notwithstanding. https://web.archive.org/web/20181226113640/http://maths.bangor.ac.uk/
I'm going to have to keep pestering the administration of the University of Leicester about this....

Would it make sense for there to be some sort of 'union' for cases like this? I Imagine that if, for example, half of the mathematicians in the UK threatened to quit in solidarity, that would be enough.
How can we create leverage as a community?

It's not the same thing, but there's a union at the University of Leicester:

UCU members ‘overwhelmingly’ back no-confidence motion in Leicester University vice-chancellor

Union members at University of Leicester have voted overwhelmingly in support of a no confidence motion in its vice-chancellor.

The University and College Union (UCU) members met yesterday to respond to the university’s proposed cuts that have left 145 staff members at risk of losing their jobs.

Some 60 people could be made redundant at the end of a current consultation and staff have been angered by vice-chancellor Professor Nishan Canagarajah’s assertion that the move is needed to “continue to deliver excellence.”

Around 200 UCU members voted in the special meeting on Monday with 98 per cent supporting the motion and no one opposing it.

Chad Nester said:

How can we create leverage as a community?

That's a good question. By the way, the last time Leicester tried to fire its mathematicians (as described by Tom Leinster in 2016), it was stopped by an international outcry, including from Sir Michael Atiyah who was actuallly Chancellor of that university between 1995 and 2005.

The UCU is exactly the organisation that has the means to oppose this sort of thing. If you're eligible to join the UCU (which mostly entails working for a university in the UK), doing so is probably by far the most useful thing you can do
The culture of unionism in universities isn't tied to subjects. People in every department will fight to help the mathematicians, and vice versa
So the UCU is an organization that links different universities? All I've read about is the "University of Leicester UCU", so I didn't know it was a branch of some bigger UCU.
Yes it's the union of academics and university workers in the UK.

The UCU is UK-wide, and is organised into a central thing and then a local chapter in each university. Technically my membership fee breaks down into separate payments to the central UCU (which includes funding political lobbying), and the local Strathclyde UCU
I think strikes and other action can legally happen both at local level and coordinated nationwide

i witnessed the UCU in action for a couple of years. it works if the union local happens to be run by a motivated and capable person. but if it is an academic, then they sacrifice their original profession, which was meant to be research and teaching, to run a political battle. moreover, they may or may not be talented and educated to run a political battle.

the other side of the UCU-management interactions is even more interesting. check a couple of principals at the UK universities. they tend to be ex-academics. if you scratch the surface, you notice that they tend to be those academics who are the first to give up their research program and become administrators. or those who never had a research program, but became academics by never leaving school. they develop a tight schedule for applying for research funding, study proposal writing and reuse, and networking. ((the research agencies are understaffed, and the program managers are overloaded and have to get rid of the money quickly, so they prefer large research networks. and the fish are inclined to biting into one another when the water for the fish soup starts getting warmer...))

so the senior academic management consists of academics who were never really interested in research, but they also never studied management or were trained to run large organizations. the worst of both worlds.

and they themselves, of course, know both about their research accomplishments and about their political acumen more than anyone. so they feel obliged to impose the sharpest performance requirements on their researchers, lest anyone thinks that they don't care for that; and they compete with one another which one will better outboris every boris in politics.

John Baez said:

The University of Leicester wants to lay off all 8 of its pure mathematicians, including all 3 women with permanent positions, and then rehire just 3 of them - to do teaching, but not research. They insanely claim:

to ensure a future research identity in AI, computational modelling, digitalisation and data science requires ceasing research in Pure Mathematics

Well, this is what happens when the people managing a university are twats that believe in the BS idea that "universities should be ran like they were companies". Pardon the French, but sometimes one has to be absolutely precise in his choice of words to convey the right meaning.
I noticed that the list of signatories is increasing and includes mathematicians from many different countries - it ought to have some impact!
Great! Hi, Marta.
Hi, John.
Welcome Marta Bunge!

A second petition has been put up for the University of Leicester's computer science department, which is affected by the proposed redundancies to a similarly devastating extent.
https://www.ipetitions.com/petition/foco-is-not-redundant/

This is if anything even more idiotic than for other departments. TCS is something that is reliably decently well funded by the UK government, the funding body EPSRC publicly (and correctly) recognise that TCS is a particular strength of UK research and say they want to keep it that way. Even someone who only cares about money and nothing else should want to keep that group around
This makes me wonder if it's actually not about money at all, but if there's someone in senior management that wants to cause as much destruction and harm as possible

From one perspective, the problem is that the university (like most) isn't run by it's faculty -- the people who fulfil the core functions of the university. Instead, the institution is controlled by a parasitic body of managers, which, in addition to creating unnecessary administrative work to justify its own existence, has little connection to the core activities of a university.

There's no real reason our universities couldn't be cooperatives run largely by the faculty and students. Apparently there's one university like this (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mondragon_University), although I haven't looked deeply into how it functions on a day-to-day basis.
I just now came across this article on the financial situation at Leicester: https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1354398863218647041.html
tl;dr they're probably right on the brink of bankruptcy and closing down entirely
It seems they've already sold all the assets they can sell, and their costs have overtaken their income, and they're already in trouble with their existing lenders
Didn't you get the Daily Mail's briefing, Jules? They're actually failing because they are “too woke” (/s)
Ironically, if what it says at the top about the European Investment Bank is true, then it's actually the Daily Heil themselves that almost single handedly got them into that situation

(This comment makes no sense to anyone who hasn't been in the UK a while, but I blame the Mail almost single handedly for brexit, certainly higher than any individual like Farage or Cummings. Almost all of our papers are right wing but the Mail is the only one I'd consider ultra-right. They used the majority of their headlines for at least the last decade to blame every problem you can imagine on "immigrants"

Now that universities are businesses, they can go bankrupt like any other business, I guess :face_palm:
It's really hard for me to articulate the depth of my frustration watching the consequences of the undermining of the higher education system in the UK of play out. Even after more of it starts to crumble, there's going to be yet another shitshow in 20 years or so when the write-off date for the first round of student loans arrives.

I don't know if this is actually right, but in my mind this is the flip side of Blair's famous target to get 50% of young people in higher education. That target was eventually reached (but during the next government, the Cameron-Clegg one, IIRC). The new funding system designed to handle that load is failing; I suspect the old system of direct funding would have failed even harder. 50% of young people in higher education seems to be right on the edge of what's economically and politically possible in the context we have

For those playing along at home, Leicester UCU have now triggered the mechanism for a formal dispute with the university. (Industrial action in the UK is governed by a set of very complicated laws that I don't understand)
Is this like a lawsuit?
My guess (I'm in Australia, so it's not quite the same) is that it is a formal process overseen by a centralised government regulator for workplace disputes that may lead to a strike if not resolved.
